A round of convection developed during the overnight and early morning hours of June 23-24 with all the ingredients in place for severe activity. An MCS traversed the northern 1/3 of the HSA producing severe wind plus heavy rain in some locations. A band of 2 to 3 inches with isolated higher amounts fell across the upper Des Moines river basin in Pocahontas and Humboldt counties, prompting the issuance of Flood Statements for expected minor flooding. The band extended northeast to the Mason City area with 1 to 2 inch amounts.  The rainfall area diminished rapidly to the southeast with little or no rainfall south of about Highway 30 across the center of Iowa.  Overnight rain gage totals amounted to 3.32" at Rolfe (RLFI4); 2.31" at Pocahontas (POCI4); Algona (AGNI4), 1.69"; Mason City (MCW), 1.54" and Britt (BTTI4), 1.50". Extremely warm and humid air surged northward across the upper Midwest with surface dew points reaching 80 degrees in a few locations on the 24th. Another in the series of nocturnal MCSs dropped some moderate rainfall in excess of one inch during the 24 hours ending at 12Z on the 25th mostly  in the upper reaches of the Des Moines basin, but the heaviest rainfall fell over far northwest Iowa. Scattered showers and thunderstorms increased in coverage throughout the morning hours of the 25th and some storms became severe with strong primarily strong winds. The very deep moisture in place, with GOES sounder estimates from 2.0 to 2.5" of precipitable water, combined with an unstable atmosphere (LIs -4 to -8) produced very efficient  rainstorms. By mid-afternoon Flood Statements for minor flooding were issued for a number of counties including Marion, Wayne, Appanoose, Warren and Polk. Antecedent conditions were quite dry across central and southern Iowa with Flash Flood Guidance over 3 inches in 1 hour in most areas. That factor combined with several breaks in the rainfall pattern prevented a potentially serious flash flood threat from developing.  At Knoxville there was two-hour break between 21 and 22Z, where very little rain fell. Street flooding was later reported in Marion County as several rural roads were overtopped with water for a brief period of time.  The maximum rainfall accumulations for one hour occurred in central Marion county where the tipping bucket rain gages at English Creek and Knoxville AWOS reaching 1.82" and 1.66". Total rainfall for 24 hours ending 12Z on the 26th  was the highest in the HSA so far this year with reports at Dallas (DLLI4) of 5.26"; Oskaloosa (OSKI4), 4.69"; Oskaloosa DCP (OOAI4), 4.67"; Indianola (IDNI4), 4.31" and Pella-KCCI (SPEI4), 4.18". Widespread 2 to 4 inch amounts fell in a band  across south central and central Iowa from Decatur County northeast through Mahaska County (see Fig. 1 below). Runoff from the heavy rain caused sharp rises on White Breast and English creeks in Marion County. The flow on English Creek rose from under 10 cfs to 1,580 cfs in only six hours, and it crested at 21.8 feet (2,859 cfs) at 16Z on the 26th.  Flooding along English Creek near Knoxville closed several local roads, prompting issuance of a Flood Statement.  Another area of 2 to 3 inch rains fell over parts of eastern Boone and western Story counties, rains which  contributed to rises on the Des Moines river at Stratford which exceeded flood stage by the morning of the 27th.   A fast moving nocturnal MCS dropped southeast across the state during the overnight hours of June 27-28, generating showers and thunderstorms with light to moderate rainfall across the HSA. The heaviest rain fell in the mid and upper Raccoon River basin, where from 1 to 1.5 inches was observed.  Highest observed amounts included 1.56" at Jefferson-KCCI (SJEI4); 1.52" at Perry (PERI4); 1.40" at Jefferson (JFFI4); 1.30" at Ames (AESI4), and 1.27" at the NWS in Johnston (DMXI4). Amounts were much lower over the south and  around 0.25 to 0.50" fell over portions of northern Iowa. The rains helped to maintain high flows along the North Raccoon River, where minor flooding continued at Jefferson and Perry crested just shy of flood stage. A dry and benign weather pattern took hold across the state from the 28th through the end of the month with pleasant temperatures and little or no rainfall.

The synoptic situation remained quite similar to that of the previous event.  A second round of convection dropped southeast into Iowa.  An MCS developed over northeast Nebraska and moved southeast into Iowa during the early morning hours.  The lead edge of the system took on the form of a bow echo as it raced into the southwest and west central counties at nearly 50 MPH.  High winds were reported with this bow echo, reaching speeds of 80 MPH west of the DMX CWA.  Winds moved into west central and southwest Iowa in the 60 to 70 MPH range, downing trees and power lines.  The bow echo lost strength as it advanced east into Iowa as the airmass was not as rich in the central counties.  Gusty winds were reported, but most were in the 40 to 50 MPH range.  One mesonet site in Boone County reported a 58 MPH wind gust with the passage of the outflow from the bow echo.  Later, on the back side of the MCS, thunderstorm activity developed on the northeast outflow boundary.  It was fed by a 50 kt low level jet and maintained itself through the night.  These storms were marginal severe in nature and produced some spotty three quarter to one inch diameter hail reports received.  One report of high winds was received from Guthrie County at Panora.

A very unstable air mass was in place over Iowa with surface dew points in the upper 70s to low 80s following a day with highs in the 90s.  Lifted indices were in the -10 to -15 C. range as the low level jet began to strengthen during the nighttime hours.  A cold front to the northwest of the state began to sink southeast into Iowa as weak low pressure moved northeast along the front.  An MCS formed near the triple point of the low and the cold front, then tracked east across northern Iowa during the early morning hours.  Scattered thunderstorms formed south of the MCS, however most of them did not produce severe weather.  As the system evolved it took on a bow echo configuration as it raced across the state.  Numerous locations reported winds of 55 to 70 MPH along the path of the storm.  Trees were blown down in many locations.  There were no reports of widespread significant damage however.  The storms moved east of the state by sunrise.  One brief tornado touchdown was reported in a field just east of Mason City.  The tornado only touched the ground before lifting and caused no damage.  One person was injured as the storms moved through the area.  High winds toppled trees in the Clear Lake area.  One of the trees fell on a woman in a tent, pinning here and causing minor injuries.  She was taken to the hospital and received 11 stitches for a head injury.

An unstable airmass was in place over Iowa during the day on the 31st, though not as unstable as was the case during the first part of the month.  Lifted indices were as low as -8 to -10 C. during the late afternoon hours over northeast Iowa with CAPE values in the 2000 to 3000 J/kg range.  Dew points were in the mid 60s to near 70 through the afternoon into the evening hours with a narrow ribbon of moisture feeding into northeast and north central Iowa through the afternoon and evening hours.  Thunderstorms fired during the afternoon, mainly northeast of the Des Moines CWA.  One storm developed in Black Hawk County and dropped golf ball size hail in the town of Dunkerton.  The weather was fairly quiet during the early evening as a cold front approached from the northwest.  Thunderstorms developed ahead of the front as the area of convection to the northeast of Iowa developed westward into north central Iowa.  The storms produced hail in several counties.  With freezing levels relatively low, at around 12,000 feet, some of the hail became quite large.  In Cerro Gordo County, hail from golf ball up to baseball size was reported in the Rockwell area.  The hail caused considerable damage.  In addition, several head of sheep were killed by the large hail on a farm near Rockwell.  In Butler, quarter to baseball size hail pelted the area.  The hail covered the ground in places resulting in near total destruction of crops south of the Dumont area.

A nearly stationary frontal boundary sank into Iowa during the afternoon hours of the 7th.  The airmass became extremely unstable with lifted indices around -12 C. and CAPE values in excess of 6000 J/kg over the south two thirds of the state.  There was little to no cap over the state by the mid day.  Convergence along the front was very strong with surface dew points rising into the upper 70s to low 80s over a large part of central Iowa by mid afternoon.  Dew points at the 850 mb level were 20 C.   As a result once thunderstorms began to form the development was explosive.  Within 30 minutes a severe super formed in central Iowa east of Des Moines.  The storms produced very large hail with numerous reports of hail larger than golf ball in size.  Softball size hail pelted the town of Newton in Jasper County.  Damage to car dealerships was high in the area.  Along with that report came several reports of baseball to softball and even larger size hail in the county.  The storm back developed into Polk County and dropped one and one half to two inch diameter hail in parts of Des Moines.  Needless to day, there was significant damage to roofs and cars in the two county area.  Once the storms weakened, additional storms developed along the outflow boundary left behind by the first storms.  Most of these storms did not become severe, however one over Marshall County did and dropped hail up to one inch in diameter near the State Center area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused severe and widespread flooding of streets and low areas. Water around two feet deep was reported at numerous locations, with one police officer reporting chest deep water as he assisted occupants of a stalled van. Numerous other vehicles were also stranded.

Once again a very unstable airmass was in place over Iowa with a weak frontal boundary extending northeast to southwest across the state an old outflow boundary extending east to west along the southern Iowa border.  The atmosphere was weakly capped during the early evening with lifted indices around -10 C and CAPE values around 4000 J/kg.  During the evening the low level jet increased to around 40 kts and thunderstorms erupted in northwest Iowa.  They were enhanced by a fast moving jet streak moving across southern Minnesota.  The thunderstorms formed into a line rather quickly and dropped very heavy rainfall of up to 2 inches per hour and hail during the early stages of the MCS development.  There were numerous reports of hail of penny size up to golf ball size in western into north central Iowa.  After midnight, the line transitioned into a wind producer and began to move southeast.  High winds of 60 to 65 MPH were common as the storms move into central and north central Iowa.  Numerous reports were received of trees and power lines downed by the storms, however damage was not widespread.

As had been the case for the past few days across Iowa, the airmass was very unstable over the state.  A stationary outflow boundary was located east to west along the southern Iowa border.  During the late afternoon heating, lifted indices reached -10 C. again with CAPE values in excess of 4000 J/kg.  Strong convergence overcame the mid level cap and thunderstorms fired just north of the surface outflow boundary.  The storms became severe rather quickly producing hail and high winds.  Most of the hail was under an inch in diameter, however golf ball size hail occurred in Adair and Wayne Counties as stronger storms moved through.  High winds caused damage in Wayne and Ringgold Counties with gusts as high as 70 MPH.  The winds downed trees and power lines.  A strong funnel cloud was spotted 1 mile northeast of Kent in Union County around 1758 CST, however it did not touch down.

The weather pattern remained similar to that it had been during the previous several days.  An east to west boundary was in position across southern Iowa with surface dew point values well into the 70s.  As the afternoon heating took place, temperatures to the south of the boundary rose to around 90.  Though the airmass was not as unstable as it had been the previous several days.  Lifted indices were around -7 C, with CAPE values around 3500 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted during the late afternoon along the boundary, producing hail and very heavy rainfall.  Golf ball size hail fell in Cass County at Anita, while one inch diameter hail fell south of Seymour in Wayne County.  Most of the other thunderstorms produced pea to marble size hail.

Unstable air remained in place over Iowa with the east west boundary remaining in place over Iowa.  South of the boundary, the airmass became very unstable with temperatures in the upper 80s and dew point readings in the 75 to 80 degree range.  It was much cooler to the north of the boundary with temperatures in the upper 60s and 70s.  At the same time, a strong upper level disturbance dropped southeast toward Minnesota toward Iowa during the afternoon.  Surface low pressure formed over Minnesota and along the cold front that extended southwest from the low.  The storms became severe quickly as they advanced into Iowa.  Winds were not a major factor in most cases with only a few gusts around 60 MPH reported.  Scattered reports of hail were received as well with most of it around an inch in diameter.  Golf ball size hail fell in Hardin County southwest of Buckeye.  The same storm produced high winds that blew a corn crib down.  One tornado occurred with these storms.  A brief touchdown occurred in Hamilton County southeast of Kamrar.  No significant damage was reported.  A bit later in the evening, as upper level system approached from the northwest, strong thunderstorms developed over southern Minnesota.  They entered Iowa during the mid evening hours, producing penny size hail and winds of up to 60 MPH.

The synoptic situation remained quite similar to that of the previous event.  A second round of convection dropped southeast into Iowa.  An MCS developed over northeast Nebraska and moved southeast into Iowa during the early morning hours.  The lead edge of the system took on the form of a bow echo as it raced into the southwest and west central counties at nearly 50 MPH.  High winds were reported with this bow echo, reaching speeds of 80 MPH west of the DMX CWA.  Winds moved into west central and southwest Iowa in the 60 to 70 MPH range, downing trees and power lines.  The bow echo lost strength as it advanced east into Iowa as the airmass was not as rich in the central counties.  Gusty winds were reported, but most were in the 40 to 50 MPH range.  One mesonet site in Boone County reported a 58 MPH wind gust with the passage of the outflow from the bow echo.  Later, on the back side of the MCS, thunderstorm activity developed on the northeast outflow boundary.  It was fed by a 50 kt low level jet and maintained itself through the night.  These storms were marginal severe in nature and produced some spotty three quarter to one inch diameter hail reports received.  One report of high winds was received from Guthrie County at Panora.

Very unstable air streamed north across Iowa during the day on the 23rd.  Temperatures warmed into the upper 80s to low 90s, with dew point readings in the upper 60s to mid 70s by the evening hours.  The upper air flow was southwesterly with a series of weak upper level disturbances moving along the flow.  The jet forcing was favorable as a speed max lifted northeast out of the trough in the southwest U.S.  A stationary front extended from northwest Minnesota southwest into Colorado.  An MCC developed during the evening over northeast Nebraska, then lifted north and east.  As the storms moved into Iowa, they produced heavy rainfall and gusty winds of 40 to 55 MPH in many areas.  One segment of the storms became severe in Sac and Calhoun Counties as a beta bow element surged ahead of the main area of thunderstorms.  Winds gusted to around 70 MPH northwest of Jolley, toppling several trees and causing some property damage.  As the storms passed south of the Pomeroy area they produced wind gusts of around 60 MPH.  In Sac County, considerable damage was done in the town of Auburn as wind of 80 to 90 MPH swept through the city.  Damage was done to the city buildings with structural damage reported.  Bricks were stripped off of the buildings, part of the roof was taken off, and numerous trees were toppled.  Debris from an old elevator tin roof near town was blown a full mile south of town by the winds.  As the storms moved east, they once again became severe as they moved into Hamilton County.  Winds around 75 MPH in the Jewell area blew over a 40 ton grain bin, smashing it against another silo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of around five inches resulted in flooding of small creeks...streets...and other low areas. The flash flooding included Pierson Creek, which rose rapidly out of its banks.

Very heavy rain caused flooding of roads...croplands...and other low areas. Over five inches of rain was reported at Aurelia and Meridan, with just less than three inches of rain in Cherokee. The flash flooding included Willow Creek, which rose rapidly out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Once again, Iowa was under very unstable conditions with lifted indices around -12 C. and cape values in the 4000 to 5000 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms were slow to fire as the 700 mb temperatures were quite warm, in the +11 to +14 C. range.  During the early morning hours a band of thunderstorms formed from north central toward central Iowa.  Most of the storms produced pea to marble size hail along with very heavy rain.  A few of the storms were of the pulse severe type.  One inch diameter hail fell west of Hampton as one storm passed overhead.  Another storm in the band spiked up and produced golf ball size hail in the city of Rockwell of far southeast Cerro Gordo County.  A short time later, one and one half inch diameter hail fell north of Waterloo from another pulse thunderstorm.  The storms then moved east of the DMX CWA.

Once again strong convection affected Iowa during the overnight hours.  A forward propagating MCS developed over the High Plains of western Nebraska into western South Dakota.  The MCS advanced east through the night.  During the early predawn hours, thunderstorms developed along the upper air warm front extending from north of Des Moines toward Sioux City.  Very heavy rainfall of 2 to 5 inches occurred along this line, though little in the way of severe weather occurred with it.  There was one storm that developed further south and dropped one inch diameter hail north of Vail in Crawford County.  Shortly before sunrise, the lead bow echo from the MCS entered the DMX CWA.  As it did, it produced pea to marble size hail and 30 to 50 MPH winds at most locations.  High winds of 60 to 70 MPH were reported in a few locations, causing damage in the form of blown down trees and power line damage.  Winds in the Grand Junction area of Greene County knocked out windows in a house there.  As the winds moved into the Ogden area of Boone County, several trees were downed on a farmstead there.  Two cars were destroyed, a 1992 Chevrolet pickup and a mid 1980s Dodge.

A round of convection developed during the overnight and early morning hours of June 23-24 with all the ingredients in place for severe activity. An MCS traversed the northern 1/3 of the HSA producing severe wind plus heavy rain in some locations. A band of 2 to 3 inches with isolated higher amounts fell across the upper Des Moines river basin in Pocahontas and Humboldt counties, prompting the issuance of Flood Statements for expected minor flooding. The band extended northeast to the Mason City area with 1 to 2 inch amounts.  The rainfall area diminished rapidly to the southeast with little or no rainfall south of about Highway 30 across the center of Iowa.  Overnight rain gage totals amounted to 3.32" at Rolfe (RLFI4); 2.31" at Pocahontas (POCI4); Algona (AGNI4), 1.69"; Mason City (MCW), 1.54" and Britt (BTTI4), 1.50". Extremely warm and humid air surged northward across the upper Midwest with surface dew points reaching 80 degrees in a few locations on the 24th. Another in the series of nocturnal MCSs dropped some moderate rainfall in excess of one inch during the 24 hours ending at 12Z on the 25th mostly  in the upper reaches of the Des Moines basin, but the heaviest rainfall fell over far northwest Iowa. Scattered showers and thunderstorms increased in coverage throughout the morning hours of the 25th and some storms became severe with strong primarily strong winds. The very deep moisture in place, with GOES sounder estimates from 2.0 to 2.5" of precipitable water, combined with an unstable atmosphere (LIs -4 to -8) produced very efficient  rainstorms. By mid-afternoon Flood Statements for minor flooding were issued for a number of counties including Marion, Wayne, Appanoose, Warren and Polk. Antecedent conditions were quite dry across central and southern Iowa with Flash Flood Guidance over 3 inches in 1 hour in most areas. That factor combined with several breaks in the rainfall pattern prevented a potentially serious flash flood threat from developing.  At Knoxville there was two-hour break between 21 and 22Z, where very little rain fell. Street flooding was later reported in Marion County as several rural roads were overtopped with water for a brief period of time.  The maximum rainfall accumulations for one hour occurred in central Marion county where the tipping bucket rain gages at English Creek and Knoxville AWOS reaching 1.82" and 1.66". Total rainfall for 24 hours ending 12Z on the 26th  was the highest in the HSA so far this year with reports at Dallas (DLLI4) of 5.26"; Oskaloosa (OSKI4), 4.69"; Oskaloosa DCP (OOAI4), 4.67"; Indianola (IDNI4), 4.31" and Pella-KCCI (SPEI4), 4.18". Widespread 2 to 4 inch amounts fell in a band  across south central and central Iowa from Decatur County northeast through Mahaska County (see Fig. 1 below). Runoff from the heavy rain caused sharp rises on White Breast and English creeks in Marion County. The flow on English Creek rose from under 10 cfs to 1,580 cfs in only six hours, and it crested at 21.8 feet (2,859 cfs) at 16Z on the 26th.  Flooding along English Creek near Knoxville closed several local roads, prompting issuance of a Flood Statement.  Another area of 2 to 3 inch rains fell over parts of eastern Boone and western Story counties, rains which  contributed to rises on the Des Moines river at Stratford which exceeded flood stage by the morning of the 27th.   A fast moving nocturnal MCS dropped southeast across the state during the overnight hours of June 27-28, generating showers and thunderstorms with light to moderate rainfall across the HSA. The heaviest rain fell in the mid and upper Raccoon River basin, where from 1 to 1.5 inches was observed.  Highest observed amounts included 1.56" at Jefferson-KCCI (SJEI4); 1.52" at Perry (PERI4); 1.40" at Jefferson (JFFI4); 1.30" at Ames (AESI4), and 1.27" at the NWS in Johnston (DMXI4). Amounts were much lower over the south and  around 0.25 to 0.50" fell over portions of northern Iowa. The rains helped to maintain high flows along the North Raccoon River, where minor flooding continued at Jefferson and Perry crested just shy of flood stage. A dry and benign weather pattern took hold across the state from the 28th through the end of the month with pleasant temperatures and little or no rainfall.

A band of 2 to 3 inches with isolated higher amounts fell across the upper Des Moines river basin in Pocahontas and Humboldt counties, prompting the issuance of Flood Statements for expected minor flooding. The band extended northeast to the Mason City area with 1 to 2 inch amounts.  The rainfall area diminished rapidly to the southeast with little or no rainfall south of about Highway 30 across the center of Iowa.  Overnight rain gage totals amounted to 3.32" at Rolfe (RLFI4); 2.31" at Pocahontas (POCI4); Algona (AGNI4), 1.69"; Mason City (MCW), 1.54" and Britt (BTTI4), 1.50". Extremely warm and humid air surged northward across the upper Midwest with surface dew points reaching 80 degrees in a few locations on the 24th. Another in the series of nocturnal MCSs dropped some moderate rainfall in excess of one inch during the 24 hours ending at 12Z on the 25th mostly  in the upper reaches of the Des Moines basin, but the heaviest rainfall fell over far northwest Iowa. Scattered showers and thunderstorms increased in coverage throughout the morning hours of the 25th and some storms became severe with strong primarily strong winds. The very deep moisture in place, with GOES sounder estimates from 2.0 to 2.5" of precipitable water, combined with an unstable atmosphere (LIs -4 to -8) produced very efficient  rainstorms. By mid-afternoon Flood Statements for minor flooding were issued for a number of counties including Marion, Wayne, Appanoose, Warren and Polk. Antecedent conditions were quite dry across central and southern Iowa with Flash Flood Guidance over 3 inches in 1 hour in most areas. That factor combined with several breaks in the rainfall pattern prevented a potentially serious flash flood threat from developing.  At Knoxville there was two-hour break between 21 and 22Z, where very little rain fell. Street flooding was later reported in Marion County as several rural roads were overtopped with water for a brief period of time.  The maximum rainfall accumulations for one hour occurred in central Marion county where the tipping bucket rain gages at English Creek and Knoxville AWOS reaching 1.82" and 1.66". Total rainfall for 24 hours ending 12Z on the 26th  was the highest in the HSA so far this year with reports at Dallas (DLLI4) of 5.26"; Oskaloosa (OSKI4), 4.69"; Oskaloosa DCP (OOAI4), 4.67"; Indianola (IDNI4), 4.31" and Pella-KCCI (SPEI4), 4.18". Widespread 2 to 4 inch amounts fell in a band  across south central and central Iowa from Decatur County northeast through Mahaska County (see Fig. 1 below). Runoff from the heavy rain caused sharp rises on White Breast and English creeks in Marion County. The flow on English Creek rose from under 10 cfs to 1,580 cfs in only six hours, and it crested at 21.8 feet (2,859 cfs) at 16Z on the 26th.  Flooding along English Creek near Knoxville closed several local roads, prompting issuance of a Flood Statement.  Another area of 2 to 3 inch rains fell over parts of eastern Boone and western Story counties, rains which  contributed to rises on the Des Moines river at Stratford which exceeded flood stage by the morning of the 27th.   A fast moving nocturnal MCS dropped southeast across the state during the overnight hours of June 27-28, generating showers and thunderstorms with light to moderate rainfall across the HSA. The heaviest rain fell in the mid and upper Raccoon River basin, where from 1 to 1.5 inches was observed.  Highest observed amounts included 1.56" at Jefferson-KCCI (SJEI4); 1.52" at Perry (PERI4); 1.40" at Jefferson (JFFI4); 1.30" at Ames (AESI4), and 1.27" at the NWS in Johnston (DMXI4). Amounts were much lower over the south and  around 0.25 to 0.50" fell over portions of northern Iowa. The rains helped to maintain high flows along the North Raccoon River, where minor flooding continued at Jefferson and Perry crested just shy of flood stage. A dry and benign weather pattern took hold across the state from the 28th through the end of the month with pleasant temperatures and little or no rainfall.

A nearly stationary frontal boundary sank into Iowa during the afternoon hours of the 7th.  The airmass became extremely unstable with lifted indices around -12 C. and CAPE values in excess of 6000 J/kg over the south two thirds of the state.  There was little to no cap over the state by the mid day.  Convergence along the front was very strong with surface dew points rising into the upper 70s to low 80s over a large part of central Iowa by mid afternoon.  Dew points at the 850 mb level were 20 C.   As a result once thunderstorms began to form the development was explosive.  Within 30 minutes a severe super formed in central Iowa east of Des Moines.  The storms produced very large hail with numerous reports of hail larger than golf ball in size.  Softball size hail pelted the town of Newton in Jasper County.  Damage to car dealerships was high in the area.  Along with that report came several reports of baseball to softball and even larger size hail in the county.  The storm back developed into Polk County and dropped one and one half to two inch diameter hail in parts of Des Moines.  Needless to day, there was significant damage to roofs and cars in the two county area.  Once the storms weakened, additional storms developed along the outflow boundary left behind by the first storms.  Most of these storms did not become severe, however one over Marshall County did and dropped hail up to one inch in diameter near the State Center area.

The synoptic situation remained quite similar to that of the previous event.  A second round of convection dropped southeast into Iowa.  An MCS developed over northeast Nebraska and moved southeast into Iowa during the early morning hours.  The lead edge of the system took on the form of a bow echo as it raced into the southwest and west central counties at nearly 50 MPH.  High winds were reported with this bow echo, reaching speeds of 80 MPH west of the DMX CWA.  Winds moved into west central and southwest Iowa in the 60 to 70 MPH range, downing trees and power lines.  The bow echo lost strength as it advanced east into Iowa as the airmass was not as rich in the central counties.  Gusty winds were reported, but most were in the 40 to 50 MPH range.  One mesonet site in Boone County reported a 58 MPH wind gust with the passage of the outflow from the bow echo.  Later, on the back side of the MCS, thunderstorm activity developed on the northeast outflow boundary.  It was fed by a 50 kt low level jet and maintained itself through the night.  These storms were marginal severe in nature and produced some spotty three quarter to one inch diameter hail reports received.  One report of high winds was received from Guthrie County at Panora.

Once again strong convection affected Iowa during the overnight hours.  A forward propagating MCS developed over the High Plains of western Nebraska into western South Dakota.  The MCS advanced east through the night.  During the early predawn hours, thunderstorms developed along the upper air warm front extending from north of Des Moines toward Sioux City.  Very heavy rainfall of 2 to 5 inches occurred along this line, though little in the way of severe weather occurred with it.  There was one storm that developed further south and dropped one inch diameter hail north of Vail in Crawford County.  Shortly before sunrise, the lead bow echo from the MCS entered the DMX CWA.  As it did, it produced pea to marble size hail and 30 to 50 MPH winds at most locations.  High winds of 60 to 70 MPH were reported in a few locations, causing damage in the form of blown down trees and power line damage.  Winds in the Grand Junction area of Greene County knocked out windows in a house there.  As the winds moved into the Ogden area of Boone County, several trees were downed on a farmstead there.  Two cars were destroyed, a 1992 Chevrolet pickup and a mid 1980s Dodge.

Sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with occasional gusts of 50 mph or even isolated wind gusts of 60 mph occurred over a large part of east central Nebraska and western Iowa during the very early morning hours on June 23.  Although thunderstorms moved across much of this same area prior to the onset of the winds, the winds occurred relatively far removed from the thunderstorms and were probably due a large part from other factors like the current surface gradient.The winds caused sporadic damage, such as downing several trees and signs.  Some relatively minor building damage was also observed.  OPPD reported that 5000 customers in east central Nebraska lost their power at the height of the storm. Other power companies over the region reported smaller outages.  Overall, however, the damage was generally not too widespread nor too severe.Although higher wind gusts were estimated to have occurred across these counties, the highest measured wind gusts included 45 mph at Tekamah and Fremont, 43 mph at Lincoln and 40 mph at Omaha Eppley-Airfield.  This was on top of sustained winds in the 30 to 40 mph range.  These strong winds lasted around 3 hours.

A very unstable air mass was in place over Iowa with surface dew points in the upper 70s to low 80s following a day with highs in the 90s.  Lifted indices were in the -10 to -15 C. range as the low level jet began to strengthen during the nighttime hours.  A cold front to the northwest of the state began to sink southeast into Iowa as weak low pressure moved northeast along the front.  An MCS formed near the triple point of the low and the cold front, then tracked east across northern Iowa during the early morning hours.  Scattered thunderstorms formed south of the MCS, however most of them did not produce severe weather.  As the system evolved it took on a bow echo configuration as it raced across the state.  Numerous locations reported winds of 55 to 70 MPH along the path of the storm.  Trees were blown down in many locations.  There were no reports of widespread significant damage however.  The storms moved east of the state by sunrise.  One brief tornado touchdown was reported in a field just east of Mason City.  The tornado only touched the ground before lifting and caused no damage.  One person was injured as the storms moved through the area.  High winds toppled trees in the Clear Lake area.  One of the trees fell on a woman in a tent, pinning here and causing minor injuries.  She was taken to the hospital and received 11 stitches for a head injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado touchdown in field just east of Mason City.
A brief tornado caused no reported damage.

An unstable airmass was in place over Iowa during the day on the 31st, though not as unstable as was the case during the first part of the month.  Lifted indices were as low as -8 to -10 C. during the late afternoon hours over northeast Iowa with CAPE values in the 2000 to 3000 J/kg range.  Dew points were in the mid 60s to near 70 through the afternoon into the evening hours with a narrow ribbon of moisture feeding into northeast and north central Iowa through the afternoon and evening hours.  Thunderstorms fired during the afternoon, mainly northeast of the Des Moines CWA.  One storm developed in Black Hawk County and dropped golf ball size hail in the town of Dunkerton.  The weather was fairly quiet during the early evening as a cold front approached from the northwest.  Thunderstorms developed ahead of the front as the area of convection to the northeast of Iowa developed westward into north central Iowa.  The storms produced hail in several counties.  With freezing levels relatively low, at around 12,000 feet, some of the hail became quite large.  In Cerro Gordo County, hail from golf ball up to baseball size was reported in the Rockwell area.  The hail caused considerable damage.  In addition, several head of sheep were killed by the large hail on a farm near Rockwell.  In Butler, quarter to baseball size hail pelted the area.  The hail covered the ground in places resulting in near total destruction of crops south of the Dumont area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust estimated at 60 mph downed a large tree in Council Bluffs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary sank into Iowa during the afternoon hours of the 7th.  The airmass became extremely unstable with lifted indices around -12 C. and CAPE values in excess of 6000 J/kg over the south two thirds of the state.  There was little to no cap over the state by the mid day.  Convergence along the front was very strong with surface dew points rising into the upper 70s to low 80s over a large part of central Iowa by mid afternoon.  Dew points at the 850 mb level were 20 C.   As a result once thunderstorms began to form the development was explosive.  Within 30 minutes a severe super formed in central Iowa east of Des Moines.  The storms produced very large hail with numerous reports of hail larger than golf ball in size.  Softball size hail pelted the town of Newton in Jasper County.  Damage to car dealerships was high in the area.  Along with that report came several reports of baseball to softball and even larger size hail in the county.  The storm back developed into Polk County and dropped one and one half to two inch diameter hail in parts of Des Moines.  Needless to day, there was significant damage to roofs and cars in the two county area.  Once the storms weakened, additional storms developed along the outflow boundary left behind by the first storms.  Most of these storms did not become severe, however one over Marshall County did and dropped hail up to one inch in diameter near the State Center area.
A thunderstorm produced isolated three quarter inch hail in far northern Buchanan County in and around the Fairbank area, causing some crop damage that affected about 50 acres.

A strong mesoscale system moved across the northern one-third of the HSA during the early morning hours of the 4th producing numerous severe thunderstorms with high winds. The fast movement of the system precluded heavy rainfall, with generally 0.50 to 0.75" across the far northern river basins and rapidly diminishing amounts southward.  Among the 24-hour rainfall reports at 12Z July 4 were 1.04" at Estherville (EST); 0.79" at Tripoli (TPLI4); 0.71" at Estherville DCP (ESVI4); 0.70" at Mason City (MCW); and 0.68" at Ringsted (RINI4). The storms did push a strong outflow boundary southward across the entire HSA , shifting winds to the north and reducing heat considerably for the 4th.  Showers and scattered storms continued through the daylight hours across the north. New convection erupted across the northern sections of the HSA during the last evening hours of the 4th, with strong to severe storms tracking west to east across north central Iowa. The heaviest rains were concentrated in the upper Raccoon River basin, where 2 to 3 inches fell in several hours and  in parts of the South Skunk basin.  Flood Statements for minor flooding were issued for Sac as well as Boone and Story counties, with street flooding reported in Ames and Boone. 24-hour rainfall at gage sites included Rockwell City (RKWI4), 2.57"; Sac City (SACI4), 2.45"; Rockwell City (RWCI4), 2.20"; Ames-KCCI (SMEI4), 2.11"; and Sac City DCP (SCRI4), 2.08". This rainfall plus forecasts of an additional rains prompted issuance of Flood Statements for moderate flooding on the North Raccoon at Jefferson and Perry. Subsequent revisions resulted in only minor flooding along the river. The active weather pattern continued on the afternoon of the July 5 as several supercell storms with large hail and strong winds developed over far southern Iowa. Rainfall of 1 to 2 inches was observed beneath the strongest storms. More strong to severe storms later moved from western Iowa into the HSA producing scattered rainfall in excess of an inch or two, and prompting issuance of a Flood Statement for minor flooding in Crawford county. Some rainfall amounts ending at 12Z on July 6 were 1.96" at Dumont (DMTI4); Humboldt (HBTI4), 1.82"; Clarion (CLII4), 1.60";  Chariton, (CHTI4), 1.50" and Osceola, (OSEI4), 1.48". Most of the HSA received a respite from rainfall for the 24 hours ending 12Z July 7, with the exception of the far northeast and a portion of the lower Raccoon River basin where scattered severe thunderstorms produce some locally moderate rainfall. Totals included Latimer-KCCI (SLMI4), 0.94"; Northwood (NWDI4), 0.92"; Panora (PANI4), 0.82"; Redfield (REDI4), 0.74", and Hartwick (HRTI4), 0.53".  Heavy rainfall and severe convective activity returned to the HSA early on July 8 as storms moved across north  central Iowa, dropping heavy rain in parts of the upper Des Moines, Iowa and Cedar basins and also in the eastern Des Moines metro area and east into Jasper county.  Prairie City (NSWI4) had 4.15"; Clarion (CLII4), 2.60"; Des Moines (STFI4),  2.41"; Bondurant (BONI4), 2.33" and Webster City (WEBI4), 2.02". Radar estimated amounts were up to 6 inches in southwest Kossuth county. More heavy rain fell during the following 24 hours as yet another mesoscale system moved across Iowa. Rainfall in this event was again centered in the upper Raccoon and Boyer river basins as 3 to 4 inches fell in an arc from Sac County southeast through Boone county. Unofficial amounts  of 5.5 inches of rain were later reported from northwest Sac county near the town of Schaller.   24-hour amounts (vt. 12Z July 9) were impressive, reaching 4.61" at Jefferson (JFFI4); 4.46" at Grand Junction (GJTI4); 3.92 at Jefferson-KCCI (SJEI4); and 3.80" and 3.57" at the two Rockwell City gages (RWCI4 and RKWI4). For this part of Iowa, a 10-year return frequency for 24-hour rainfall is about 4.40" and the 5-year return about 3.75".   Fairly heavy rains of 1 to possibly 3 inches were also observed along the southern tier of counties east of Interstate 35. There was considerable road damage in Sac County, with up to $95,000 later reported by county maintenance personnel.The extremely stormy pattern was finally brought to an end by a slow-moving cool front that pushed southeast across the state on July 9 bringing scattered thunderstorms, severe weather and some scatted moderate rains. Fortunately, very little fell in the saturated grounds of the North Raccoon River basin. The highest totals were over the far southeast and a small area of the middle Des Moines basin Report included Bloomfield (BMFI4), 1.50"; Gilman (GLMI4), 1.30"; Blockton (BLKI4), 1.19"; Webster City (WEBI4), 1.10" and Ames (AESI4) ,1.07". The extended period of rainfall brought rivers in the central part of Iowa to flood stage and above, especially in the Raccoon River basin. The North Raccoon River experienced the highest flows due to the concentration of the heaviest rainfall in the upper portions of the basin mainly across Sac and Calhoun counties. The stage at Lanesboro (LKCI4) on the North Raccoon was the 2nd highest in a 9-year period of record at 18.50 feet and Grand Junction (GJTI4) on Buttrick Creek, a tributary of the North Raccoon, reached 17.71 feet, the highest in 9 years of record.  At Perry (PROI4) the river reached 18.78 feet on the 10th, the 5th highest crest and  highest since 1993. This prompted issuance of a Flood Warning for the river in that area. The flood crest on the North Raccoon attenuated substantially as it joined the Middle Raccoon River since there was substantially less rainfall in the Middle and South Raccoon basins. Still, minor flooding was observed along the mainstem Raccoon through Des Moines, cresting at Fleur Drive (DEMI4) on the 13th at 13.49 feet (Flood Stage is 12 feet). This was merely 13 feet lower than 10 years ago almost to the day when the river crested at 26.7 feet on July 11, 1993.

Unofficial amounts  of 5.5 inches of rain were later reported from northwest Sac county near the town of Schaller.   24-hour amounts (vt. 12Z July 9) were impressive, reaching 4.61" at Jefferson (JFFI4); 4.46" at Grand Junction (GJTI4); 3.92 at Jefferson-KCCI (SJEI4); and 3.80" and 3.57" at the two Rockwell City gages (RWCI4 and RKWI4). For this part of Iowa, a 10-year return frequency for 24-hour rainfall is about 4.40" and the 5-year return about 3.75".   Fairly heavy rains of 1 to possibly 3 inches were also observed along the southern tier of counties east of Interstate 35. There was considerable road damage in Sac County, with up to $95,000 later reported by county maintenance personnel.The extremely stormy pattern was finally brought to an end by a slow-moving cool front that pushed southeast across the state on July 9 bringing scattered thunderstorms, severe weather and some scatted moderate rains. Fortunately, very little fell in the saturated grounds of the North Raccoon River basin. The highest totals were over the far southeast and a small area of the middle Des Moines basin Report included Bloomfield (BMFI4), 1.50"; Gilman (GLMI4), 1.30"; Blockton (BLKI4), 1.19"; Webster City (WEBI4), 1.10" and Ames (AESI4) ,1.07". The extended period of rainfall brought rivers in the central part of Iowa to flood stage and above, especially in the Raccoon River basin. The North Raccoon River experienced the highest flows due to the concentration of the heaviest rainfall in the upper portions of the basin mainly across Sac and Calhoun counties. The stage at Lanesboro (LKCI4) on the North Raccoon was the 2nd highest in a 9-year period of record at 18.50 feet and Grand Junction (GJTI4) on Buttrick Creek, a tributary of the North Raccoon, reached 17.71 feet, the highest in 9 years of record.  At Perry (PROI4) the river reached 18.78 feet on the 10th, the 5th highest crest and  highest since 1993. This prompted issuance of a Flood Warning for the river in that area. The flood crest on the North Raccoon attenuated substantially as it joined the Middle Raccoon River since there was substantially less rainfall in the Middle and South Raccoon basins. Still, minor flooding was observed along the mainstem Raccoon through Des Moines, cresting at Fleur Drive (DEMI4) on the 13th at 13.49 feet (Flood Stage is 12 feet). This was merely 13 feet lower than 10 years ago almost to the day when the river crested at 26.7 feet on July 11, 1993.

A strong mesoscale system moved across the northern one-third of the HSA during the early morning hours of the 4th producing numerous severe thunderstorms with high winds. The fast movement of the system precluded heavy rainfall, with generally 0.50 to 0.75" across the far northern river basins and rapidly diminishing amounts southward.  Among the 24-hour rainfall reports at 12Z July 4 were 1.04" at Estherville (EST); 0.79" at Tripoli (TPLI4); 0.71" at Estherville DCP (ESVI4); 0.70" at Mason City (MCW); and 0.68" at Ringsted (RINI4). The storms did push a strong outflow boundary southward across the entire HSA , shifting winds to the north and reducing heat considerably for the 4th.  Showers and scattered storms continued through the daylight hours across the north. New convection erupted across the northern sections of the HSA during the last evening hours of the 4th, with strong to severe storms tracking west to east across north central Iowa. The heaviest rains were concentrated in the upper Raccoon River basin, where 2 to 3 inches fell in several hours and  in parts of the South Skunk basin.  Flood Statements for minor flooding were issued for Sac as well as Boone and Story counties, with street flooding reported in Ames and Boone. 24-hour rainfall at gage sites included Rockwell City (RKWI4), 2.57"; Sac City (SACI4), 2.45"; Rockwell City (RWCI4), 2.20"; Ames-KCCI (SMEI4), 2.11"; and Sac City DCP (SCRI4), 2.08". This rainfall plus forecasts of an additional rains prompted issuance of Flood Statements for moderate flooding on the North Raccoon at Jefferson and Perry. Subsequent revisions resulted in only minor flooding along the river. The active weather pattern continued on the afternoon of the July 5 as several supercell storms with large hail and strong winds developed over far southern Iowa. Rainfall of 1 to 2 inches was observed beneath the strongest storms. More strong to severe storms later moved from western Iowa into the HSA producing scattered rainfall in excess of an inch or two, and prompting issuance of a Flood Statement for minor flooding in Crawford county. Some rainfall amounts ending at 12Z on July 6 were 1.96" at Dumont (DMTI4); Humboldt (HBTI4), 1.82"; Clarion (CLII4), 1.60";  Chariton, (CHTI4), 1.50" and Osceola, (OSEI4), 1.48". Most of the HSA received a respite from rainfall for the 24 hours ending 12Z July 7, with the exception of the far northeast and a portion of the lower Raccoon River basin where scattered severe thunderstorms produce some locally moderate rainfall. Totals included Latimer-KCCI (SLMI4), 0.94"; Northwood (NWDI4), 0.92"; Panora (PANI4), 0.82"; Redfield (REDI4), 0.74", and Hartwick (HRTI4), 0.53". There was considerable road damage in Sac County, with up to $95,000 later reported by county maintenance personnel.The extremely stormy pattern was finally brought to an end by a slow-moving cool front that pushed southeast across the state on July 9 bringing scattered thunderstorms, severe weather and some scatted moderate rains. Fortunately, very little fell in the saturated grounds of the North Raccoon River basin. The highest totals were over the far southeast and a small area of the middle Des Moines basin Report included Bloomfield (BMFI4), 1.50"; Gilman (GLMI4), 1.30"; Blockton (BLKI4), 1.19"; Webster City (WEBI4), 1.10" and Ames (AESI4) ,1.07". The extended period of rainfall brought rivers in the central part of Iowa to flood stage and above, especially in the Raccoon River basin. The North Raccoon River experienced the highest flows due to the concentration of the heaviest rainfall in the upper portions of the basin mainly across Sac and Calhoun counties. The stage at Lanesboro (LKCI4) on the North Raccoon was the 2nd highest in a 9-year period of record at 18.50 feet and Grand Junction (GJTI4) on Buttrick Creek, a tributary of the North Raccoon, reached 17.71 feet, the highest in 9 years of record.  At Perry (PROI4) the river reached 18.78 feet on the 10th, the 5th highest crest and  highest since 1993. This prompted issuance of a Flood Warning for the river in that area. The flood crest on the North Raccoon attenuated substantially as it joined the Middle Raccoon River since there was substantially less rainfall in the Middle and South Raccoon basins. Still, minor flooding was observed along the mainstem Raccoon through Des Moines, cresting at Fleur Drive (DEMI4) on the 13th at 13.49 feet (Flood Stage is 12 feet). This was merely 13 feet lower than 10 years ago almost to the day when the river crested at 26.7 feet on July 11, 1993.

A nearly stationary frontal boundary sank into Iowa during the afternoon hours of the 7th.  The airmass became extremely unstable with lifted indices around -12 C. and CAPE values in excess of 6000 J/kg over the south two thirds of the state.  There was little to no cap over the state by the mid day.  Convergence along the front was very strong with surface dew points rising into the upper 70s to low 80s over a large part of central Iowa by mid afternoon.  Dew points at the 850 mb level were 20 C.   As a result once thunderstorms began to form the development was explosive.  Within 30 minutes a severe super formed in central Iowa east of Des Moines.  The storms produced very large hail with numerous reports of hail larger than golf ball in size.  Softball size hail pelted the town of Newton in Jasper County.  Damage to car dealerships was high in the area.  Along with that report came several reports of baseball to softball and even larger size hail in the county.  The storm back developed into Polk County and dropped one and one half to two inch diameter hail in parts of Des Moines.  Needless to day, there was significant damage to roofs and cars in the two county area.  Once the storms weakened, additional storms developed along the outflow boundary left behind by the first storms.  Most of these storms did not become severe, however one over Marshall County did and dropped hail up to one inch in diameter near the State Center area.
A line of thunderstorms from Waterloo Iowa to Galena Illinois produced pulse severe storms that resulted in localized high wind and hail damage during the afternoon.

Flood Statements for minor flooding were issued for Sac as well as Boone and Story counties, with street flooding reported in Ames and Boone. 24-hour rainfall at gage sites included Rockwell City (RKWI4), 2.57"; Sac City (SACI4), 2.45"; Rockwell City (RWCI4), 2.20"; Ames-KCCI (SMEI4), 2.11"; and Sac City DCP (SCRI4), 2.08". This rainfall plus forecasts of an additional rains prompted issuance of Flood Statements for moderate flooding on the North Raccoon at Jefferson and Perry. Subsequent revisions resulted in only minor flooding along the river. The active weather pattern continued on the afternoon of the July 5 as several supercell storms with large hail and strong winds developed over far southern Iowa. Rainfall of 1 to 2 inches was observed beneath the strongest storms. More strong to severe storms later moved from western Iowa into the HSA producing scattered rainfall in excess of an inch or two, and prompting issuance of a Flood Statement for minor flooding in Crawford county. Some rainfall amounts ending at 12Z on July 6 were 1.96" at Dumont (DMTI4); Humboldt (HBTI4), 1.82"; Clarion (CLII4), 1.60";  Chariton, (CHTI4), 1.50" and Osceola, (OSEI4), 1.48". Most of the HSA received a respite from rainfall for the 24 hours ending 12Z July 7, with the exception of the far northeast and a portion of the lower Raccoon River basin where scattered severe thunderstorms produce some locally moderate rainfall. Totals included Latimer-KCCI (SLMI4), 0.94"; Northwood (NWDI4), 0.92"; Panora (PANI4), 0.82"; Redfield (REDI4), 0.74", and Hartwick (HRTI4), 0.53".  Heavy rainfall and severe convective activity returned to the HSA early on July 8 as storms moved across north  central Iowa, dropping heavy rain in parts of the upper Des Moines, Iowa and Cedar basins and also in the eastern Des Moines metro area and east into Jasper county.  Prairie City (NSWI4) had 4.15"; Clarion (CLII4), 2.60"; Des Moines (STFI4),  2.41"; Bondurant (BONI4), 2.33" and Webster City (WEBI4), 2.02". Radar estimated amounts were up to 6 inches in southwest Kossuth county. More heavy rain fell during the following 24 hours as yet another mesoscale system moved across Iowa. Rainfall in this event was again centered in the upper Raccoon and Boyer river basins as 3 to 4 inches fell in an arc from Sac County southeast through Boone county. Unofficial amounts  of 5.5 inches of rain were later reported from northwest Sac county near the town of Schaller.   24-hour amounts (vt. 12Z July 9) were impressive, reaching 4.61" at Jefferson (JFFI4); 4.46" at Grand Junction (GJTI4); 3.92 at Jefferson-KCCI (SJEI4); and 3.80" and 3.57" at the two Rockwell City gages (RWCI4 and RKWI4). For this part of Iowa, a 10-year return frequency for 24-hour rainfall is about 4.40" and the 5-year return about 3.75".   Fairly heavy rains of 1 to possibly 3 inches were also observed along the southern tier of counties east of Interstate 35. There was considerable road damage in Sac County, with up to $95,000 later reported by county maintenance personnel.The extremely stormy pattern was finally brought to an end by a slow-moving cool front that pushed southeast across the state on July 9 bringing scattered thunderstorms, severe weather and some scatted moderate rains. Fortunately, very little fell in the saturated grounds of the North Raccoon River basin. The highest totals were over the far southeast and a small area of the middle Des Moines basin Report included Bloomfield (BMFI4), 1.50"; Gilman (GLMI4), 1.30"; Blockton (BLKI4), 1.19"; Webster City (WEBI4), 1.10" and Ames (AESI4) ,1.07". The extended period of rainfall brought rivers in the central part of Iowa to flood stage and above, especially in the Raccoon River basin. The North Raccoon River experienced the highest flows due to the concentration of the heaviest rainfall in the upper portions of the basin mainly across Sac and Calhoun counties. The stage at Lanesboro (LKCI4) on the North Raccoon was the 2nd highest in a 9-year period of record at 18.50 feet and Grand Junction (GJTI4) on Buttrick Creek, a tributary of the North Raccoon, reached 17.71 feet, the highest in 9 years of record.  At Perry (PROI4) the river reached 18.78 feet on the 10th, the 5th highest crest and  highest since 1993. This prompted issuance of a Flood Warning for the river in that area. The flood crest on the North Raccoon attenuated substantially as it joined the Middle Raccoon River since there was substantially less rainfall in the Middle and South Raccoon basins. Still, minor flooding was observed along the mainstem Raccoon through Des Moines, cresting at Fleur Drive (DEMI4) on the 13th at 13.49 feet (Flood Stage is 12 feet). This was merely 13 feet lower than 10 years ago almost to the day when the river crested at 26.7 feet on July 11, 1993.

A nearly stationary frontal boundary sank into Iowa during the afternoon hours of the 7th.  The airmass became extremely unstable with lifted indices around -12 C. and CAPE values in excess of 6000 J/kg over the south two thirds of the state.  There was little to no cap over the state by the mid day.  Convergence along the front was very strong with surface dew points rising into the upper 70s to low 80s over a large part of central Iowa by mid afternoon.  Dew points at the 850 mb level were 20 C.   As a result once thunderstorms began to form the development was explosive.  Within 30 minutes a severe super formed in central Iowa east of Des Moines.  The storms produced very large hail with numerous reports of hail larger than golf ball in size.  Softball size hail pelted the town of Newton in Jasper County.  Damage to car dealerships was high in the area.  Along with that report came several reports of baseball to softball and even larger size hail in the county.  The storm back developed into Polk County and dropped one and one half to two inch diameter hail in parts of Des Moines.  Needless to day, there was significant damage to roofs and cars in the two county area.  Once the storms weakened, additional storms developed along the outflow boundary left behind by the first storms.  Most of these storms did not become severe, however one over Marshall County did and dropped hail up to one inch in diameter near the State Center area.

Once again strong convection affected Iowa during the overnight hours.  A forward propagating MCS developed over the High Plains of western Nebraska into western South Dakota.  The MCS advanced east through the night.  During the early predawn hours, thunderstorms developed along the upper air warm front extending from north of Des Moines toward Sioux City.  Very heavy rainfall of 2 to 5 inches occurred along this line, though little in the way of severe weather occurred with it.  There was one storm that developed further south and dropped one inch diameter hail north of Vail in Crawford County.  Shortly before sunrise, the lead bow echo from the MCS entered the DMX CWA.  As it did, it produced pea to marble size hail and 30 to 50 MPH winds at most locations.  High winds of 60 to 70 MPH were reported in a few locations, causing damage in the form of blown down trees and power line damage.  Winds in the Grand Junction area of Greene County knocked out windows in a house there.  As the winds moved into the Ogden area of Boone County, several trees were downed on a farmstead there.  Two cars were destroyed, a 1992 Chevrolet pickup and a mid 1980s Dodge.

As had been the case for the past few days across Iowa, the airmass was very unstable over the state.  A stationary outflow boundary was located east to west along the southern Iowa border.  During the late afternoon heating, lifted indices reached -10 C. again with CAPE values in excess of 4000 J/kg.  Strong convergence overcame the mid level cap and thunderstorms fired just north of the surface outflow boundary.  The storms became severe rather quickly producing hail and high winds.  Most of the hail was under an inch in diameter, however golf ball size hail occurred in Adair and Wayne Counties as stronger storms moved through.  High winds caused damage in Wayne and Ringgold Counties with gusts as high as 70 MPH.  The winds downed trees and power lines.  A strong funnel cloud was spotted 1 mile northeast of Kent in Union County around 1758 CST, however it did not touch down.

The synoptic situation remained quite similar to that of the previous event.  A second round of convection dropped southeast into Iowa.  An MCS developed over northeast Nebraska and moved southeast into Iowa during the early morning hours.  The lead edge of the system took on the form of a bow echo as it raced into the southwest and west central counties at nearly 50 MPH.  High winds were reported with this bow echo, reaching speeds of 80 MPH west of the DMX CWA.  Winds moved into west central and southwest Iowa in the 60 to 70 MPH range, downing trees and power lines.  The bow echo lost strength as it advanced east into Iowa as the airmass was not as rich in the central counties.  Gusty winds were reported, but most were in the 40 to 50 MPH range.  One mesonet site in Boone County reported a 58 MPH wind gust with the passage of the outflow from the bow echo.  Later, on the back side of the MCS, thunderstorm activity developed on the northeast outflow boundary.  It was fed by a 50 kt low level jet and maintained itself through the night.  These storms were marginal severe in nature and produced some spotty three quarter to one inch diameter hail reports received.  One report of high winds was received from Guthrie County at Panora.

Unstable air remained in place over Iowa with the east west boundary remaining in place over Iowa.  South of the boundary, the airmass became very unstable with temperatures in the upper 80s and dew point readings in the 75 to 80 degree range.  It was much cooler to the north of the boundary with temperatures in the upper 60s and 70s.  At the same time, a strong upper level disturbance dropped southeast toward Minnesota toward Iowa during the afternoon.  Surface low pressure formed over Minnesota and along the cold front that extended southwest from the low.  The storms became severe quickly as they advanced into Iowa.  Winds were not a major factor in most cases with only a few gusts around 60 MPH reported.  Scattered reports of hail were received as well with most of it around an inch in diameter.  Golf ball size hail fell in Hardin County southwest of Buckeye.  The same storm produced high winds that blew a corn crib down.  One tornado occurred with these storms.  A brief touchdown occurred in Hamilton County southeast of Kamrar.  No significant damage was reported.  A bit later in the evening, as upper level system approached from the northwest, strong thunderstorms developed over southern Minnesota.  They entered Iowa during the mid evening hours, producing penny size hail and winds of up to 60 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down briefly in a field southeast of Kamrar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage, especially in the northern part of Sioux City. Several trees were blown down, and numerous limbs and smaller tree debris were blown down and littered streets and yards. Power lines were blown down, and resulting power outages affected an estimated 2,500 customers. The roof of a commercial building housing multiple businesses was blown off. Several homes sustained roof damage, and the porch of a home was damaged.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including some trees and large branches blown down.
A brief tornado caused no reported damage.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree and power line damage. Several trees were blown down, including a large tree which blocked U.S. Highway 59. The downed power lines resulted in power outages.
Lightning caused a house fire which damaged the roof and ceiling of a home.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including several trees blown down.

Needless to day, there was significant damage to roofs and cars in the two county area.  Once the storms weakened, additional storms developed along the outflow boundary left behind by the first storms.  Most of these storms did not become severe, however one over Marshall County did and dropped hail up to one inch in diameter near the State Center area.

A nearly stationary frontal boundary sank into Iowa during the afternoon hours of the 7th.  The airmass became extremely unstable with lifted indices around -12 C. and CAPE values in excess of 6000 J/kg over the south two thirds of the state.  There was little to no cap over the state by the mid day.  Convergence along the front was very strong with surface dew points rising into the upper 70s to low 80s over a large part of central Iowa by mid afternoon.  Dew points at the 850 mb level were 20 C.   As a result once thunderstorms began to form the development was explosive.  Within 30 minutes a severe super formed in central Iowa east of Des Moines.  The storms produced very large hail with numerous reports of hail larger than golf ball in size.  Softball size hail pelted the town of Newton in Jasper County.  Damage to car dealerships was high in the area.  Along with that report came several reports of baseball to softball and even larger size hail in the county.  The storm back developed into Polk County and dropped one and one half to two inch diameter hail in parts of Des Moines.  Needless to day, there was significant damage to roofs and cars in the two county area.  Once the storms weakened, additional storms developed along the outflow boundary left behind by the first storms.  Most of these storms did not become severe, however one over Marshall County did and dropped hail up to one inch in diameter near the State Center area.

A strong mesoscale system moved across the northern one-third of the HSA during the early morning hours of the 4th producing numerous severe thunderstorms with high winds. The fast movement of the system precluded heavy rainfall, with generally 0.50 to 0.75" across the far northern river basins and rapidly diminishing amounts southward.  Among the 24-hour rainfall reports at 12Z July 4 were 1.04" at Estherville (EST); 0.79" at Tripoli (TPLI4); 0.71" at Estherville DCP (ESVI4); 0.70" at Mason City (MCW); and 0.68" at Ringsted (RINI4). The storms did push a strong outflow boundary southward across the entire HSA , shifting winds to the north and reducing heat considerably for the 4th.  Showers and scattered storms continued through the daylight hours across the north. New convection erupted across the northern sections of the HSA during the last evening hours of the 4th, with strong to severe storms tracking west to east across north central Iowa. The heaviest rains were concentrated in the upper Raccoon River basin, where 2 to 3 inches fell in several hours and  in parts of the South Skunk basin.  Flood Statements for minor flooding were issued for Sac as well as Boone and Story counties, with street flooding reported in Ames and Boone. 24-hour rainfall at gage sites included Rockwell City (RKWI4), 2.57"; Sac City (SACI4), 2.45"; Rockwell City (RWCI4), 2.20"; Ames-KCCI (SMEI4), 2.11"; and Sac City DCP (SCRI4), 2.08". This rainfall plus forecasts of an additional rains prompted issuance of Flood Statements for moderate flooding on the North Raccoon at Jefferson and Perry. Subsequent revisions resulted in only minor flooding along the river. The active weather pattern continued on the afternoon of the July 5 as several supercell storms with large hail and strong winds developed over far southern Iowa. Rainfall of 1 to 2 inches was observed beneath the strongest storms. More strong to severe storms later moved from western Iowa into the HSA producing scattered rainfall in excess of an inch or two, and prompting issuance of a Flood Statement for minor flooding in Crawford county. Some rainfall amounts ending at 12Z on July 6 were 1.96" at Dumont (DMTI4); Humboldt (HBTI4), 1.82"; Clarion (CLII4), 1.60";  Chariton, (CHTI4), 1.50" and Osceola, (OSEI4), 1.48". Most of the HSA received a respite from rainfall for the 24 hours ending 12Z July 7, with the exception of the far northeast and a portion of the lower Raccoon River basin where scattered severe thunderstorms produce some locally moderate rainfall. Totals included Latimer-KCCI (SLMI4), 0.94"; Northwood (NWDI4), 0.92"; Panora (PANI4), 0.82"; Redfield (REDI4), 0.74", and Hartwick (HRTI4), 0.53".  Heavy rainfall and severe convective activity returned to the HSA early on July 8 as storms moved across north  central Iowa, dropping heavy rain in parts of the upper Des Moines, Iowa and Cedar basins and also in the eastern Des Moines metro area and east into Jasper county.  Prairie City (NSWI4) had 4.15"; Clarion (CLII4), 2.60"; Des Moines (STFI4),  2.41"; Bondurant (BONI4), 2.33" and Webster City (WEBI4), 2.02". Radar estimated amounts were up to 6 inches in southwest Kossuth county. More heavy rain fell during the following 24 hours as yet another mesoscale system moved across Iowa. Rainfall in this event was again centered in the upper Raccoon and Boyer river basins as 3 to 4 inches fell in an arc from Sac County southeast through Boone county. Unofficial amounts  of 5.5 inches of rain were later reported from northwest Sac county near the town of Schaller.   24-hour amounts (vt. 12Z July 9) were impressive, reaching 4.61" at Jefferson (JFFI4); 4.46" at Grand Junction (GJTI4); 3.92 at Jefferson-KCCI (SJEI4); and 3.80" and 3.57" at the two Rockwell City gages (RWCI4 and RKWI4). For this part of Iowa, a 10-year return frequency for 24-hour rainfall is about 4.40" and the 5-year return about 3.75".   Fairly heavy rains of 1 to possibly 3 inches were also observed along the southern tier of counties east of Interstate 35. There was considerable road damage in Sac County, with up to $95,000 later reported by county maintenance personnel.The extremely stormy pattern was finally brought to an end by a slow-moving cool front that pushed southeast across the state on July 9 bringing scattered thunderstorms, severe weather and some scatted moderate rains. Fortunately, very little fell in the saturated grounds of the North Raccoon River basin. The highest totals were over the far southeast and a small area of the middle Des Moines basin Report included Bloomfield (BMFI4), 1.50"; Gilman (GLMI4), 1.30"; Blockton (BLKI4), 1.19"; Webster City (WEBI4), 1.10" and Ames (AESI4) ,1.07". The extended period of rainfall brought rivers in the central part of Iowa to flood stage and above, especially in the Raccoon River basin. The North Raccoon River experienced the highest flows due to the concentration of the heaviest rainfall in the upper portions of the basin mainly across Sac and Calhoun counties. The stage at Lanesboro (LKCI4) on the North Raccoon was the 2nd highest in a 9-year period of record at 18.50 feet and Grand Junction (GJTI4) on Buttrick Creek, a tributary of the North Raccoon, reached 17.71 feet, the highest in 9 years of record.  At Perry (PROI4) the river reached 18.78 feet on the 10th, the 5th highest crest and  highest since 1993. This prompted issuance of a Flood Warning for the river in that area. The flood crest on the North Raccoon attenuated substantially as it joined the Middle Raccoon River since there was substantially less rainfall in the Middle and South Raccoon basins. Still, minor flooding was observed along the mainstem Raccoon through Des Moines, cresting at Fleur Drive (DEMI4) on the 13th at 13.49 feet (Flood Stage is 12 feet). This was merely 13 feet lower than 10 years ago almost to the day when the river crested at 26.7 feet on July 11, 1993.
